# Appendix B. Identifiability Results {#sec-appendix-b .unnumbered}

> *The four results below are stated at the level of rigor the foundations
> currently support: B.1 and B.3 are proofs under explicit genericity
> conditions; B.2 is a numerically-supported proposition (a Fisher-information /
> near-kernel claim, not a strict invariance theorem); B.4 is bookkeeping once
> B.3 is in place.*

## B.1 Identifiability of $\alpha$ from $\eta$ {#sec-b1 .unnumbered}

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Proposition B.1.** Let $\eta = (\eta_r)_{r \in \mathcal{R}}$ be fixed. Suppose
there is a menu $\mathcal{M} \subseteq \mathcal{R}$ of positive design
probability and two alternatives $r, s \in \mathcal{M}$ with $\eta_r \neq
\eta_s$. Then the map $\alpha \mapsto P(\cdot \mid \alpha, \eta)$ restricted to
$\mathcal{M}$ is injective on $(0, \infty)$; hence $\alpha$ is globally
identifiable from choice probabilities on $\mathcal{M}$.
:::

*Proof.* On $\mathcal{M}$ the softmax (@eq-softmax) gives, for the pair $r, s$,
$$
g(\alpha) \;:=\; \log\frac{P(r \mid \alpha, \eta)}{P(s \mid \alpha, \eta)}
  \;=\; \alpha\,(\eta_r - \eta_s).
$$
Since $\eta_r - \eta_s \neq 0$, $g$ is strictly monotone (linear with nonzero
slope) in $\alpha$, hence injective: distinct $\alpha$ values induce distinct
log-odds, therefore distinct choice-probability vectors on $\mathcal{M}$. As the
menu has positive design probability, the choice distribution determines
$g(\alpha)$ and thus $\alpha$. $\qquad\blacksquare$

*Remark (genericity).* The condition "not all $\eta_r$ equal on some
positive-probability menu" replaces the vague "sufficient feature diversity": it
is exactly the condition under which softmax curvature in $\alpha$ is non-trivial.
It fails only in the measure-zero event that every presented menu has perfectly
tied expected utilities.

## B.2 Weak (practical) identifiability of $(\beta,\delta)$ in `m_0` {#sec-b2 .unnumbered}

This result is a **Fisher-information / curvature** claim, established
numerically by the spike `spikes/b2_fisher_block_spike.py`
(see `spikes/B2_RESULTS.md`), not a strict $\eta$-preserving group.

**Setup.** At a parameter value $(\alpha, \beta, \delta)$ and the study design of
Appendix D.0 (here $K = 3$, $D = 5$, $R = 15$, $M = 50$, normal features), the
expected per-design $(\beta,\delta)$ Fisher block is
$$
I \;=\; \sum_{m} G_m^\top \big(\mathrm{diag}(p_m) - p_m p_m^\top\big) G_m,
\qquad G_m = \alpha\,\frac{\partial \eta_m}{\partial(\beta,\delta)},
\qquad p_m = \mathrm{softmax}(\alpha\,\eta_m).
$$
The analytic $\eta$-Jacobian was verified against central finite differences (max
absolute error $1.0\times10^{-10}$). The exact $\beta$ row-shift gauge (adding a
common $\gamma \in \mathbb{R}^D$ to every row of $\beta$; $D$ directions) was
confirmed to be exactly $\eta$-preserving and Fisher-null ($\leq 1.6\times10^{-16}$)
and projected out before measuring the ridge.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Proposition B.2.** After removing the exact $\beta$ gauge, the
$(\beta,\delta)$ Fisher block $I$ is strongly ill-conditioned at representative
parameter values and the design's sample sizes: its condition number satisfies
$\kappa = \lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min} \gtrsim 10^3$. The Schur complement
isolating $\delta$,
$$
I_{\delta\delta}^{\text{prof}} = I_{\delta\delta} - I_{\delta\beta}
  I_{\beta\beta}^{+} I_{\beta\delta},
$$
retains only a fraction $0.01$–$0.37$ of the marginal $\delta$ information
$I_{\delta\delta}$. The flattest gauge-fixed direction changes within-menu
$\eta$-contrasts --- hence choice probabilities --- by only 2–8% of a typical
direction. There is therefore a near-flat, approximately
choice-probability-preserving direction in $(\beta,\delta)$-space at realistic
$n$, and no strict $\eta$-preserving group beyond the separately-handled $\beta$
gauge.
:::

**Numerical evidence.** At four representative prior draws ($\alpha \in
\{0.45, 0.88, 0.60, 0.64\}$):

| draw | $\alpha$ | $\kappa$ | $\delta$ Schur ratio | flat-dir choice-prob sensitivity / median |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | 0.45 | $1.4\times10^{3}$ | 0.014 | 0.083 |
| 2 | 0.88 | $2.8\times10^{4}$ | 0.367 | 0.023 |
| 3 | 0.60 | $4.2\times10^{3}$ | 0.280 | 0.059 |
| 4 | 0.64 | $3.9\times10^{4}$ | 0.034 | 0.032 |

An $\alpha$ sweep $\in \{0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10\}$ at fixed $(\beta,\delta)$ gives
$\kappa \approx 1.4$–$2.1\times10^{3}$, Schur ratio $\approx 0.009$–$0.014$, and
choice-probability ratio $\approx 0.08$–$0.15$ --- all three conclusions robust
to $\alpha$.

**Reading.** (i) The ridge exists ($\kappa \gtrsim 10^3$, with a low-curvature
tail spanning three to four orders of magnitude, not a single isolated null).
(ii) Profiling out $\beta$ destroys 63–99% of $\delta$'s information --- the
precise computational form of the multiplicative $(\beta,\delta)$ coupling
documented by parameter recovery (Report 4) and the matched-design study
(Report 14). (iii) Because the flat direction preserves choice probabilities and
$\alpha$ is identified from those (B.1), the ridge leaves $\alpha$ untouched
(§[3.5](#sec-m0-link)). Note that the flattest single direction is predominantly
$\beta$ (a near-extension of the gauge), with $\delta$-weight $\approx 0$;
$\delta$'s weak identification is therefore reported via the Schur ratio, not by
reading it off the smallest eigenvector.

## B.3 Global identifiability of $\delta$ in `m_1` {#sec-b3 .unnumbered}

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Proposition B.3.** Let the risky problems present lotteries from a set
$\{\pi_s\}_{s=1}^{S} \subset \Delta^{K-1}$ whose elements are *affinely
independent* (which requires $S \geq K$ and a generic configuration). Then, with
$\alpha > 0$ known to be positive and the endpoint convention $\upsilon_1 = 0$,
$\upsilon_K = 1$ in force, the utility vector $\bm{\upsilon}$ --- and hence
$\delta$ --- is globally identified from risky-choice probabilities alone.
:::

*Proof.* By Proposition B.1 (applied to the risky menus), $\alpha$ and, for each
risky menu, the within-menu contrasts of $\eta^{(r)}_s = \pi_s^\top \bm{\upsilon}$
are determined by the choice probabilities. Thus the differences $(\pi_s -
\pi_{s'})^\top \bm{\upsilon}$ are identified for lotteries appearing in a common
menu, and by chaining menus over the design, the linear functionals
$\{\pi_s^\top \bm{\upsilon}\}$ are identified up to a single common additive
constant. Stack the affinely independent lotteries: affine independence means the
vectors $\{\pi_s - \pi_1\}_{s=2}^{S}$ span the $(K-1)$-dimensional tangent space
of the simplex, so the linear map
$$
L : \bm{\upsilon} \longmapsto \big((\pi_s - \pi_1)^\top \bm{\upsilon}\big)_{s=2}^{S}
$$
has rank $K-1$ and is injective on the $(K-1)$-dimensional space of
endpoint-constrained utilities. Hence the identified contrasts determine
$\bm{\upsilon}$ uniquely, and the cumulative-sum map $\delta \mapsto
\bm{\upsilon}$ being a bijection onto the ordered-utility set, $\delta$ is
identified. $\qquad\blacksquare$

*Remark.* This is the cleanest of the four results: it is a finite-dimensional
linear inversion, with the only substantive hypothesis being affine independence
of the presented lotteries --- a design condition the experimenter controls.

## B.4 Identifiability of $\beta$ modulo the row-shift gauge {#sec-b4 .unnumbered}

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Proposition B.4.** Suppose $\delta$ (hence $\bm{\upsilon}$) is identified by
B.3 and $\alpha > 0$ by B.1. Then $\beta$ is identified from the uncertain-choice
probabilities modulo the additive row-shift gauge of §[2.6](#sec-notation),
provided the feature design $\{w_r\}$ is of full rank $D$ and the induced
expected utilities are non-constant across some positive-probability menus.
:::

*Proof (bookkeeping).* With $\alpha$ and $\bm{\upsilon}$ fixed, the
uncertain-choice probabilities determine the within-menu contrasts of $\eta_r =
\bm{\psi}_r^\top \bm{\upsilon}$ (Proposition B.1), hence --- as $\bm{\upsilon}$ is
known and the menus vary --- the expected utilities $\eta_r = \bm{\psi}_r^\top
\bm{\upsilon}$ up to the usual common constant per menu. Generic variation of the
features $w_r$ across alternatives makes the map $\bm{\psi} \mapsto (\eta_r)_r$
injective on the relevant region (the utilities $\bm{\upsilon}$ are not constant
across consequences once $\delta$ is interior), so the simplices $\bm{\psi}_r$
are recovered. Finally, $\bm{\psi}_r = \mathrm{softmax}(\beta w_r)$ with
full-rank features determines $\beta$ up to the additive row-shift gauge, which
is the exact invariance of the belief softmax and cannot be resolved from choice
data. $\qquad\blacksquare$
